coredata.dc:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) doped ZnFe2O4/TiO2 hybrid materials with high photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yida
Feng, Yiping
Wang, Fengliang
Zou, Xuegang
Chen, Zhi-Feng
Chen, Ping
Liu, Haijin
Su, Yuehan
Zhang, Qianxin
Liu, Guoguang
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, a novel and highly efficient ternary photocatalytic ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method. The properties and structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR), and photoluminescence spectrum (PL). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was used to demonstrate that carbon quantum dots could increase the absorption of visible light wavelengths. When compared with TiO2/3.00wt%CDs and ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanocomposites, the ternary photocatalysts exhibited the enhanced photocatalytic activity under solar-spectrum irradiation. A low CDs content of 3.00wt% in the ternary photocatalysts possessed excellent performance in the decomposition of Rhodamine B (RhB). Act as donator and receiver, CDs can efficiently improve the ability of electron transfer and separate the electron-hole pairs. OH and O2
− were confirmed to be involved in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB base on the Electron spin resonance (ESR) tests, and the photocatalytic mechanism of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/3.00wt%CDs was proposed. After four sequential operational cycles, the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs maintained its photocatalytic efficiency, indicating its high photocatalytic stability. Therefore, our results provide a high efficient photocatalytic nanocomposite that synthetized by a facile method, and have implications in the removal of environmental persistent pollutants under ternary photocatalysts mediated solar degradation
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
TiO2
ZnFe2O4
Rhodamine
Charge separation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image, (b) EDS image, (c)–(e) TEM images and (f) HRTEM image of ZTC3.00.

(a) XRD patterns of (1) pure ZnFe2O4, (2) pure TiO2, (3) ZnFe2O4/TiO2 composites, (4) ZTC3.00composites, (5) ZTC11.6 composites, (6) 25wt%ZTC3.00 composites, (b) FT-IR of (1) pure ZnFe2O4, (2) pure TiO2, (3) ZnFe2O4/TiO2, (4) ZTC3.00.
(a) XPS spectra of the Survey of ZTC3.00 hybrid samples (b) Ti 2P; (c) Fe 2P; (d) C 1S.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (DRS) of (1) pure ZnFe2O4, (2) pure TiO2, (3) TiO2/3.00wt%CQDs, (4) ZnFe2O4/TiO2, (5) ZTC3.00 (b) The PL emission spectra (kex=425nm) of as-prepared photocatalysts (1) pure TiO2, (2) ZnFe2O4/TiO2, (3) ZTC3.00, (4) pure ZnFe2O4.

(a) Adsorption and photodegradation of RhB in the presence of all samples, (Reaction conditions: RhB=10mg/L, T=25°) (b) Relation between all catalysts and the kinetic constant of RhB. (c) The recycled photocatalytic experiments of ZTC3.00 sample for degradation of RhB (d) XRD patterns for the ZTC3.00 composite photocatalyst before and after the photocatalytic reaction.
DMPO-ESR spin-trapping spectra of ZTC3.00. (a) Superoxide radical (b) Hydroxyl radical.
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism of ZTC3.00.

head.ce:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) doped ZnFe2O4/TiO2 hybrid materials with high photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, No. 100 Waihuan Xi Road, Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Center, Guangzhou, 510006, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphical abstract shows the schematic photocatalytic mechanism of RhB (a target pollutant for the ZTC3.00 composites during the simulated sunlight irradiation photocatalytic process. The ZTC3.00 composites performs excellent photocatalytic activity, believing that a promising technology in reducing the water pollutants in the further.

In this study, a novel and highly efficient ternary photocatalytic ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method. The properties and structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR), and photoluminescence spectrum (PL). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was used to demonstrate that carbon quantum dots could increase the absorption of visible light wavelengths. When compared with TiO2/3.00wt%CDs and ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanocomposites, the ternary photocatalysts exhibited the enhanced photocatalytic activity under solar-spectrum irradiation. A low CDs content of 3.00wt% in the ternary photocatalysts possessed excellent performance in the decomposition of Rhodamine B (RhB). Act as donator and receiver, CDs can efficiently improve the ability of electron transfer and separate the electron-hole pairs. OH and O2
− were confirmed to be involved in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB base on the Electron spin resonance (ESR) tests, and the photocatalytic mechanism of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/3.00wt%CDs was proposed. After four sequential operational cycles, the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs maintained its photocatalytic efficiency, indicating its high photocatalytic stability. Therefore, our results provide a high efficient photocatalytic nanocomposite that synthetized by a facile method, and have implications in the removal of environmental persistent pollutants under ternary photocatalysts mediated solar degradation

ce:section.ce:para: With the advantages of cost-effectiveness, environmental compatibility, stability and high efficiency, TiO2 nanomaterials are considered to be a superior photocatalyst for air pollutant degradation, organic sewage reduction, and household waste sterilization [1,2]. Nevertheless, there are numerous elements limit the photocatalytic efficiency of titanium dioxide including limited reusability, the requirement of UV light for activation, rapid recombination and possession of wide band gap (3.2eV). Furthermore, particle agglomeration could cause photocatalytic material to deactivate easily in photodegradation process [3]. Hence, it cannot be widely used in practical applications [4]. In order to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanomaterials, multiple studies have proposed to dope TiO2 composite, which has been widely considered. Doping metals and non-metals into TiO2 nanomaterial is a practicable way to narrow the band gap energy, which makes electronic transition of catalysts active from the valence band under sunlight [5–7]. Therefore, improving the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 powders demands to modify nanocomposite photocatalysts appropriately. Possessing a low band gap (1.92eV), good magnetic separation characteristics, with chemical stability at low-cost, spinel zinc ferrite nonmaterial (ZnFe2O4) is regarded as an excellent candidate for photocatalytic activities under light irradiation [8,9]. As a well-known photocatalytic material, ZnFe2O4 nanomaterials are compounded in hydrothermal synthesis [10], solvothermal [11], and sol-gel process [12], etc. Although electrons are easily excited in the ZnFe2O4 material exposure to sunlight, rapid electron-hole pair recombination in ZnFe2O4 results in poor photocatalytic performance [13,14].

It is acknowledged that carbon dots (CDs) not only have chemical stability and sensitivity to visible light, but also possess the advantages of unique photoluminescence (PL) upconversion property and non-toxicity [15]. Recently, carbon dots have been widely investigated as an excellent candidate for photo-induced processes, and integration into nanocomposite photocatalysts [16]. It was observed by Duo that CDs/BiOBr composites demonstrated higher photocatalytic activity than did pure CDs or BiOBr, due to the upconversion effect of the CDs [17]. The incorporation of CDs can improve photocatalytic activity, while accommodating the adsorption of longer photocatalyst wavelength [18]. CDs may generate additional electrons and decrease the recombination of electron-hole pairs, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic activity [15]. Therefore, the application of CDs for the doping of photocatalysts toward the degradation of dye contaminants is a promising prospect for water purification.

Concurrent with continuously expanding economic and social development, the issue of water pollution, particularly dye pollution, has gaining increased attention in recent years [19]. It has been reported that over 70,000 tons of dyes are discharged into industrial wastewaters annually [20]. Moreover, it is difficult to degrade dye infused waste-water due to the high concentrations of dyes and their chemical complexity [21]. More importantly, stable dyes have a significant negative influence on ecological balance and myriad biological life forms, including human beings; causing cancer and other diseases, when they are discharged into the aqueous environment [20]. The discovery of solutions to dye contamination is urgent and critical [22,23]. Many researchers are dedicated to the development of photocatalytic technologies, which are well-known for their utility in the degradation of dye permeated wastewater.

In the present work, we synthesized CDs-doped ZnFe2O4/TiO2 samples for the purpose of improving the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper describing the preparation of ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs nanocomposites. This work not only aimed to address the drawbacks of ZnFe2O4 and TiO2 (P25) nanomaterials, but also to demonstrate and verify the superior performance of CDs. The optimal mass ratio of CDs doped ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanocomposites content for the Rhodamine B photodegradation activity was determined. Further, the structures, morphologies, and properties of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs nanocomposites were systematically evaluated by state-of-the-art characterization techniques. Finally, the potential photocatalytic mechanism of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs was proposed.

ZnFe2O4, TiO2 (P25) and Citric acid (analytically pure), were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Ethanediamine (98% purity) was purchased from Tianjin TaiMau Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China); Alcohol (95% purity), was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China); Ultra-pure water used in all experiments was generated by a dedicated pure water system (Smart 2 MilliQ apparatus, TKA, Germany).

In this experiment, CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method [24], where 0.50g citric acid was dissolved in a measured volume (30.00mL) of ultra-pure water under magnetic stirring. Following 60min of stirring, 2.50mL of ethanediamine was added to the Beaker, which again underwent magnetic stirring for 60 mins. The mixed solution was subsequently transferred to a 100.00mL sealed Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and allowed heat for 300min at 180°C. The obtained CDs solution was separated with a centrifuge, after cooling to room temperature, in order to remove molecular particulates. The concentration of CDs was 15mg/L.

The ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method[25]. In a typical procedure, 500.00mg TiO2, 15.00mg ZnFe2O4, and certain quantities (0.5mL, 1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL) of CDs were put into 30.00mL ethanol. Subsequently, the solution was mixed by magnetic stirring for 6h before undergoing evaporation at 70°C to dry. The mixture was then transferred to a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 1.5°C/min. and maintained at 300°C for 2h. Once the muffle furnace naturally cooled to room temperature, the resulting catalysts were obtained by grinding for 30min using an agate mortar. The added contents of CDs to the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs hybrid nanomaterials were 1.16wt%, 3.00wt%, 5.80wt%, 8.70wt%, and 11.6wt%, which were mark ZTC1.16, ZTC3.00, ZTC5.80, ZTC8.70, and ZTC11.6, respectively. The ZnFe2O4/TiO2 and TiO2/3.00wt%CDs nanoparticles were synthesized using the same process without the addition of CDs and ZnFe2O4.

For the purpose of characterizing the phase structures and particle dimensions of all samples, X-ray power diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Rigaku D/max-Ultima X-ray diffractometer (Cu Ka radiation (λ=1.54156A)), operating at 40KV and 40mA, at a scan rate of 8°/min., from 10° to 80°. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-vis DRS) of samples were determined with a UV–vis absorption spectrometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu); The infrared spectra of the as-prepared samples were recorded by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometers (FT-IR) (Thermofisher Nicolet 6700) in the scan range of between 400 and 4000cm-1.; Additionally, a scanning electron microscope (FESEM; Hitachi S-4800, Japan) was employed to observe the morphologies of the catalysts, whereas a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) was used to examine the structure of the samples. Further, Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (HORIBA EX-250) was also used to examine the samples during the FESEM measurements. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to measure functional groups, and the chemical composition was elucidated with a spectrometer (Thermo, ESCALAB 250Xi). The PL spectra of the photocatalysts were also surveyed by Fluorescence Spectroscopy (F97 PRO, Lengguang Tech). The ESR spectra of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/3.00wt%CDs were validated by Electron Spin Resonance spectroscopy (Bruker, E500).

The photocatalytic activities of the prepared nanocomposites were examined via the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in an aqueous solution. The experiment was carried out using the photoreactor (XPA-7, Nanjing XUJ Co. Ltd.). Pyrex and quartz tubes were vertically placed in the photoreaction device, which included a quartz cooling jacket [26]. The light source employed was a 350W Xe lamp, which possessed full spectral distribution, with a 290nm cut off filter so as to simulate sunlight irradiation. For each experiment, 50.00mg of as-synthesized catalysts was dispersed in a 50.00mL RhB aqueous solution (10.00mg/L) in a cylindrical quartz tube under ultrasonication. In order to ensure the attainment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the RhB and the prepared nanomaterials, the resultant solution was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 mins. Subsequently, the light was turned on and the photoreactor was illuminated under magnetic stirring (1000rpm). At certain time intervals (15min), 3.00mL samples were extracted; whereafter the catalyst was removed using a needle filter (SCAA-101). Afterward, the concentrations of RhB were analyzed by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV759S) at a specific wavelength (554nm). The effects of RhB in all samples were investigated under identical conditions.

SEM, EDS, and TEM of the ZTC3.00 photocatalysts were employed to investigate their morphologies and microstructures, with the images displayed in Fig. 1 . The image in Fig. 1 (a) depicts the morphology of the ZTC3.00, which was revealed to be in the size range of 500nm. The ZTC3.00 nanocomposite presented spherical structure with dimensions of 500nm. The nanomaterials observed in the image appear very similar as the diameters of TiO2 and ZnFe2O4 were almost equivalent. Moreover, the elements of the samples are shown in Fig. 1 (b). The EDS pattern further revealed the existence of Ti, Zn, Fe, O, and C in the sample, where Ti was prominent among all elements. In particular, it may also be observed that the Ag content was the next most prevalent, as conductive silver adhesive was used to affix the samples. In addition, it was anticipated that the C content would be third highest due to the presence of the CDs. To further investigate the structure of the photocatalysts, TEM analysis was conducted. The structure of the nanocomposite in Fig. 1 (c), (d), (e) and (f), was revealed using TEM imagery. These images revealed the structural and morphological of ZTC3.00 nanocomposite that the CDs were adhered to the layer of TiO2 and dispersed TiO2 nanoparticles. This image verified that CDs deposited the ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanocomposite surface, which indicated that they were successfully doped within the samples. The high resolution TEM image in Fig. 1 (f) further showed the lattice fringes (d spacing) of hybrid photocatalysts have been measured to be 0.22nm, 0.342nm and 0.245nm in consistence with the interplanar spacing of the plane of CDs, TiO2 and ZnFe2O4, respectively.

The XRD patterns of pure TiO2, ZnFe2O4/TiO2, ZTC3.00, ZTC11.6 and 25wt%ZnFe2O4/TiO2/3.00wt%CDs were identified in Fig. 2 (a). According to Fig. 2 (a), the characteristic peaks of pure ZnFe2O4 (spinel phase) coincided with the ZnFe2O4 data (JCPDS CARD No: 79-1150) [27]. Most of the hybrid material was comprised of TiO2, with anatase crystal structure (JCPDS CARD 04-0477). It is depicted in Fig. 2 that characteristic peaks TiO2 were located at 2θ=25.8°, 37.82°，48.07°, 53.94°，55.05°, 62.74°, 70.34°, and 75.16°. These data suggested that the TiO2 was suitable as the anatase TiO2; hence, determined the crystal faces of the TiO2 to be (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (220), and (215), respectively[28–30]. It was calculated, as shown in Fig. 2, using Scherer’s equation that the crystallite size of anatase TiO2 was 20.1nm while for ZnFe2O4 it was 25 nm[25]. As compared to the XRD pattern of samples in Fig. 2 (a), no other peaks and impurities were observed in the ZTC3.00 sample because of the small content of ZnFe2O4 (Zn: Ti=0.01:1, mole ratio) and CDs (3.00wt%). The crystalline peak of ZTC3.00 nanocomposite corresponds to the anatase TiO2 crystalline structure. When we increased the CDs content of sample, no further characteristic peaks appeared, as seen in Fig. 2. Moreover, it may be clearly seen in Fig. 2 (a) that diffraction peaks of the 25wt%ZnFe2O4/TiO2/3.00wt%CDs nanocomposite were detected at 2θ=30.4°, 35.7°, 42.97° and 57.1° due to the mass ratio of ZnFe2O4 at 25%. Therefore, we confirmed that the CDs did not disturb the XRD pattern of the ZTC3.00 hybrid nanomaterial, whereas a small quantity of ZnFe2O4 had a positive effect in the photocatalysts.

In order to further study the attributes of the catalysts, the infrared spectra were acquired, as shown in Fig. 2. The presence of the band at 3430cm−1 and 1630cm−1, which represented the presence of an OH group, was observed in all samples. In Fig. 2, the absorption bands at 571cm−1 belonging to ZnFe2O4 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the FeO band [27]. Broad bands about 600–800cm−1 regions in Fig. 2b (123) may be in agreement with the TiOTi stretching vibrations. Two small and narrow absorption peaks were identified as the characteristic absorption peak of the doped CDs, which might corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the CH and CO bands in 1000–1500cm−1 the region[31,32]. In addition, the new absorption peaks of the nanomaterials were primarily ascribed to the effects of the interactions between the ZnFe2O4/TiO2 composite and CDs, which translated to the CDs being successfully doped within the catalysts, as verified by the presence of the new chemical bands.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been widely used to analyse the chemical compositions and bonding characteristics of photocatalysts. For the hybrid materials, the presence of elements Ti, Zn, Fe, O, and adventitious C are shown in Fig. 3 . The atomic ratios of Ti, O, and C in the sample were 24.61%, 54.93% and 19.42%, respectively. Basis on the EDS data, it was 6.67% C content in the sample due to the natural contamination reason. Fig. 3 indicates that Ti 2P characteristic peaks appeared at 458.7eV and 464.4eV, which were attributed to Ti 2P3/2 and Ti 2P1/2. High-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S1) for the Zn 2P indicated two peaks at 1021.0eV and 1044.2eV, which were assigned to Zn 2P1/2 and Zn 2P3/2. Further, peaks with binding energies of 724.4eV and 711.0eV should be corresponded to Fe 2P1/2 and Fe 2P3/2, whose content was negligible in the compound. Moreover, the high resolution O 1s peaks could be divided into three peaks. It was observed that the primary peak was located at 530.5eV, which was ascribed to TiO. The other O 1s peak positions (Fig. S2) in the ZTC3.00 sample at 531.8eV, and 532.9eV, were attributed to CO and HOCO 19,33,34]. As is shown in Fig. 3, the three peaks were respectively 287.8, 286.7, and 283.7eV, and assigned to COH, CO, and CO [34]. Hence, 19.42% carbon content was estimated to through analysis to exist in the ZTC3.00 sample due to the integration of the CDs. Significantly, on the basis of the XPS characterization analysis it was verified new functional groups emerged owing to the successfully doped CDs.

The optical properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts, as well as the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were characterized, and are shown in Fig. 3 (a). The absorption properties of were measured in the range from 300 to 700nm. As depicted in the inset of Fig. 4 (a), the absorption peak was 380nm, as TiO2 serves as an absorption element. As shown in Fig. 3 (a) the absorption wavelength of pure ZnFe2O4 was observed in the visible range, where the absorbance slightly declined, from 300nm to 700nm, while the pure TiO2 was present in the UV region. The pure anatase TiO2 was the lowest in all samples, and the 3.00wt%CDs coating in the TiO2 hybrid material exhibited a slight increase in absorbance. Further, the spectrum of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanomaterial was higher than the TiO2/3.00wt%CDs photocatalysts. In addition, the absorption spectra of ZTC3.00 hybrid material was more robust than the ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanomaterial owing to the presence of CDs, which may have assist to increase the population of electron-hole pairs under simulated sunlight irradiation [35,36]. Thus, the CDs might led to a red shift in the absorption wavelength of solar light, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic degradation activities of the samples.

To further elucidate and demonstrate the process of charge carrier trapping, migration transfer and separation transfer, the photoluminescence (PL) technique was performed for this experiment. It was worth investigating whether a CD coating might display emission characteristics. Further, the excitation wavelength was varied, from 300nm to 500nm. As depicted in Fig. 4 (b), the optimal emission wavelength of all samples was centered at ∼425nm. In Fig. 4 (b), the TiO2 generated the fluorescence highest intensity value. The decreased intensity of TiO2/3.00wt%CDs may have contributed to the TiO2 being affected by the CDs. As indicated in Fig. 3 (b), the primary emission peak of the sample was altered in comparison with the other nanomaterials. Furthermore, it was observed that the ZTC3.00 nanocomposite exhibited the lowest photoluminescence intensity, which indicated the enhancement of the electron-hole pair separation capacity and demonstrated the optimal photocatalytic capacity over the unmodified ZnFe2O4/TiO2 nanocomposite [37–39]. On one hand, the CDs had the capacity to enhance the electron-hole pair separation ability of photocatalysts and increase the photocatalytic performance [40]. On the other hand, the surfaces of the CDs had more influence in delaying the photoluminescent recombination of electrons and holes [41]. Conversely, the presence of CDs led to more highly efficient electron transfer though the low content of CDs. In conclusion, doping with CDs served to augment the spectral properties of the ZTC3.00, which translated to lowing the photoluminescence intensity of the hybrid nanomaterials.

As a target pollutant, RhB is typically employed to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples under simulated solar light irradiation. The catalyst (0.05g) was firstly dispersed in RhB aqueous solution (50mL, 10mg/L, T=25°C). Prior to the photocatalytic reaction, there was no degradation evident under magnetic stirring for 30min or even up to 105min in the dark. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), RhB underwent negligible degradation in a blank experiment, as well as with pure ZnFe2O4. However, Fig. 5 (a) reveals that nearly 90% of the RhB was photodegraded with ZTC3.00 samples under simulated sunlight irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (b), the ZTC3.00 displayed the highest photocatalytic activity over the other samples for the degradation of RhB. The RhB degradation coincided with pseudo-first-order kinetics and its rate (A) was calculated as follows (1) and (2) [42,43]:
(1) In (C/C0)=−kt
(2) A%=(1−C/C0)×100%
Where C0
is the initial UV–vis absorption, C is the UV–vis absorption following photolysis, k is the pseudo-first-order kinetic constant, t is the irradiation time, and C is certain constant, respectively.

To comprehend the behavior of RhB degradation, the kinetic constant K was further computed in Fig. 5 (b). The reaction rate constant of RhB was 0.1362 for the ZTC3.00 nanomaterial subsequent to 105min of solar light irradiation. According to its photocatalytic activity, it may be seen that the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs sample exhibited the optimal photocatalytic efficacy when the Carbon Dots quantity attained 3.00wt%. In contrast, with increasing concentrations of CDs, the photocatalytic ability of the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs composites became progressively weaker. This is due to the increasing population of CDs that are loaded onto the surface of ZnFe2O4/TiO2, which acts to restrict the ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs from absorbing more light [44].

The ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs samples were removed using a centrifuge process, rinsed three times with pure water and dried for recycling. What can be seen in Fig. 5 (c) are the recycling times of the ZTC3.00 nanocomposite during the photocatalytic RhB degradation process. As is shown in Fig. 5 (d), it can be seen that XRD patterns for the ZTC3.00 composite photocatalyst before and after the photocatalytic reaction. It was observed that the performance of the ZTC3.00 sample in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB not diminished following four run times, which indicated that the photocatalyst maintained high stability and good photocatalytic performance.

In order to explore the potential photodegradative reactive species that were generated by the ZTC3.00 sample, DMPO-ESR spin-trapping spectra of samples were recorded, as shown in Fig. 6. The ESR test is an excellent technique for the determination of the primary degradation kinetics degradation under simulated solar irradiation [31,45]. As depicted in Fig. 6 , we determined that the most reactive species were hydroxyl and superoxide radicals. The center field of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals is ∼318.45mT. In darkness, there were no reactive oxygen species present, as shown in Fig. 6. It is worth mentioning that the populations of OH and O2
− radicals were generated in an aqueous solution when the irradiation time was five mins. What’s more, it was observed that the intensity of the OH and O2
− radicals increased rapidly in the ensuing five mins. This directly inferred that OH and O2
− radicals were indeed generated during the photocatalytic process. Interestingly, the intensity of O2
− radicals attained almost 700, whereas the OH radicals were close to 5000. Therefore, as seen in Fig. 6, it may directly confirm that the OH and O2
− radicals were likely the two primary possible reactive oxygen species that were involved in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB.

On the strength of the experimental results and some literatures [12,42,46–52], the proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB over ZTC3.00 catalysts under simulated sunlight irradiation was proposed, as displayed in Fig. 7 . It is recognized that reactive oxygen species (OH, O2
−, e−, h+ and H2O2) were involved in the degradation process [47]. The ESR analysis verified that the OH and O2
− radicals were two primary possible active species candidates. Based on the formulas EVB =X-Ee +0.5Eg and EVB =ECB +Eg, where EVB is the valence band potentials, ECB is the conduction band potential of sample, Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale, and X is the electronegativity of the corresponding semiconductor. Zinc ferrite is the initiator of the entire photocatalytic system. Initially, by virtue of a low band gap (1.92eV), once the samples were exposed to simulated sunlight, electrons from the VB (0.38eV) of ZnFe2O4 (Eg=1.92eV) may be easily excited to the CB (−1.54eV), alongside leaving the generation of holes (h+) in the VB of ZnFe2O4. As known, n-type semiconductor ZnFe2O4 can be easily coupled with an intrinsic p-type semiconductor anatase TiO2 to form heterojunction structure which exhibits the excellent properties of charge carrier trapping, migration, transfer and separation transfer [53,54]. Subsequently, the electrons tend to transfer more to the VB of TiO2 (Eg=3.20eV) and generate new holes which could initiate photocatalytic oxidation reactions in the VB of TiO2. It is worth mentioning that Z-scheme electron transfer can occur in the two narrow photocatalysts. Through an electron mediator, the low CB of ZnFe2O4 can generate electrons which transfer to the high CB of TiO2. In heterostructured photocatalytic system, it can be increase the mobility of photogenerated electrons [55]. Moreover, the enhanced of ZTC3.00 performance under simulated light irradiation can attribute to the effect of CDs. On the one hand, act as a bridge, the presence of CDs modified on the surface to improve the interfacial electron transfer ability of TiO2. The electrons on the CB of TiO2 can efficiently transfer to the CDs to inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, more carbonyl groups and aldehyde groups which can attack the structure of pollutants were formed [48,51,56]. With increasing electrons populations, more O2
− radicals were generated from the O2
−Subsequently, some O2
− radicals reacted with H2O in aqueous solution and were eventually transformed to OH. Thus, the higher concentration of superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals participate in the photodegradation reaction could be obtained in the ZTC3.00 system than another sample under simulated light irradiation. Concomitantly, the RhB molecules were absorbed by leaving holes (h+) in the ZnFe2O4, thus RhB+* was generated following the reaction. Under the action of reactive oxygen species, RhB was ultimately degraded to carbon dioxide and water. Hence, the proposed mechanism was exhibited in Fig. 7 and as follows:
(3) ZnFe2O4/TiO2/CDs+hv→ZnFe2O4 (h+)+TiO2/CDs (e−, h+)
(4) e− +O2 →O2
−
(5) h+ +H2O→ OH+H+
(6) O2
− +e−+ H+ → OH+OH−
(7) ZnFe2O4 (h+)+Rh B→Rh B+
(8) Rh B+ →CO2 +H2O
(9) Rh B+hv→Rh B*→Rh B+*+e−
(10) ROS+Rh B→Intermediates→CO2 +H2O

It was demonstrated for the first time that a ZTC3.00 successfully developed doping process by way of a hydrothermal method. The characterization of the photocatalysts were analysed by XRD, DRS, PL, FT-IR, SEM, TEM, EDS, and XPS techniques. The photodegradation activities of ZTC3.00 may be superior to those of ZnFe2O4, TiO2, TiO2/3.00wt%CDs, or ZnFe2O4/TiO2. These ternary nanocomposites demonstrated robust photodegradation activities, through which over 90% of the target RhB was removed under light illumination for 105 mins. The heterostructure of the samples, which accelerated the transport of electrons and the separation of electron-hole pairs to enhance photocatalytic performance were explored in our experiments. We deduced that the two primary active species were OH and O2
− radicals. Due to limitations of the experimental conditions, it is beyond the scope of this paper to comprehensively articulate the full range of nuanced attributes and activities of these nanophotocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of our work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21377031 and 21677040), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No.2016A030313697), the Innovative Team Program of High Education of Guangdong Province(2015KCXTD007), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2017A050506052) and the Science and Technology Research Key Project of Education Department of Henan Province (13A610528)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2017.10.049.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
